subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_text: On what ground can you say that scandium (Z = 21) is a transition element but zinc (Z = 30) is not?
question_html: On what ground can you say that scandium (Z = 21) is a transition element but zinc (Z = 30) is not?
solution_text: For an element to be a transition element, it should have incomplete filled d-subshell in ground state or in most common oxidation state. Sc in its ground state has incompletely filled d-orbitals (3d 1 ,4s 2 ). The orbitals in the zinc (3d 10 4s 2 )are completely filled in the ground state as well as in their common oxidation states. Therefore, Sc is regarded as transition element but Zn is not regarded as transition element.
solution_html: For an element to be a transition element, it should have incomplete filled d-subshell in ground state or in most common oxidation state. Sc in its ground state has incompletely filled d-orbitals (3d1,4s2). The orbitals in the zinc (3d104s2)are completely filled in the ground state as well as in their common oxidation states. Therefore, Sc is regarded as transition element but Zn is not regarded as transition element.

question_text: Why do the transition elements exhibit higher enthalpies of atomization?
question_html: Why do the transition elements exhibit higher enthalpies of atomization?
solution_text: Transition elements have a large number of unpaired electrons in their atoms they have stronger interatomic interaction and hence stronger metallic bonding between atoms resulting in the higher enthalpies of atomization.

question_text: Silver atom has completely filled d orbitals (4d 10 ) in its ground state. How can you say that it is a transition element?
question_html: Silver atom has completely filled d orbitals (4d10) in its ground state. How can you say that it is a transition element?
solution_text: Ag has a completely filled 4d orbital ( 4d 10 5s 1 ) in its ground state. Now, silver displays two oxidation states (+1 and +2). In stable +2 oxidation state, one electron is removed from outermost 5s orbital as well as one electron is also removed from 4d-orbital. Thus, the valence shell configuration of Ag +2 will be (4d 9 5s 0 ). The d-orbital now becomes incomplete (4d 9 ). Hence, it is a transition element.
solution_html: Ag has a completely filled 4d orbital ( 4d105s1) in its ground state. Now, silver displays two oxidation states (+1 and +2). In stable +2 oxidation state, one electron is removed from outermost 5s orbital as well as one electron is also removed from 4d-orbital. Thus, the valence shell configuration of Ag+2 will be (4d9 5s0). The d-orbital now becomes incomplete (4d9). Hence, it is a transition element.

question_text: In the series Sc (Z = 21) to Zn (Z = 30) the enthalpy of atomization of Zinc is the lowest, i.e. 126 kJ mol -1 Why?
question_html: In the series Sc (Z = 21) to Zn (Z = 30) the enthalpy of atomization of Zinc is the lowest, i.e. 126 kJ mol-1 Why?
solution_text: In the series Sc to Zn all elements have one or more unpaired electron except zinc which has no unpaired electron as its outer electronic configuration is 3d 10 4s 2 Hence interatomic metallic bonding is weakest in zinc. Therefore, enthalpy of atomization is lowest.

question_text: Which of the 3d series of the transition metals exhibits the largest number of oxidation states and why?
question_html: Which of the 3d series of the transition metals exhibits the largest number of oxidation states and why?
solution_text: Mn (Z = 25) has valence shell configuration as 3d 5 4s 2 . Mn has the maximum number of unpaired electrons present in the d-subshell (5 electrons). Hence, Mn exhibits the largest number of oxidation states, ranging from +2 to +7. +2 oxidation state is shown by Mn when outermost 2 electrons are removed from 4s orbital. Further in +3 to +7 oxidation state one by one the unpaired electrons are removed from 3d orbital.

+2 oxidation state is shown by Mn when outermost 2 electrons are removed from 4s orbital.
Further in +3 to +7 oxidation state one by one the unpaired electrons are removed from 3d orbital.

question_text: The E o (M 2+ /M) value for copper is positive (+0.34V). What is possibly the reason for this?
question_html: The Eo (M2+/M) value for copper is positive (+0.34V). What is possibly the reason for this?
solution_text: The E o (M 2+ /M) value of a metal depends on the energy changes involved in the following: Sublimation: The energy required for converting one mole of an atom from the solid state to the gaseous state. {tex}{M_{(s)}} \to {M_{(g)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\Delta _s}H(Sub\lim ation\,\,energy){/tex} Ionization: The energy required to take out electrons from one mole of atoms in the gaseous state. {tex}{M_{(g)}} \to {M^{2 + }} + 2e^- _{(g)}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\Delta _i}H(Ionization\,\,energy){/tex} Hydration: The energy released when one mole of ions are hydrated. {tex}{M^{2 + }}_{(g)} \to {M^{2 + }}_{(aq)}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\Delta _{hyd}}H(Hydration\,\,energy){/tex} Now, copper has a high energy of sublimation and very high sum of first two ionization energy values and low hydration energy. Hence, the E o ​​​​​​​(M 2+ /M) value for copper is positive.
solution_html:
The Eo(M2+/M) value of a metal depends on the energy changes involved in the following:

  1. Sublimation: The energy required for converting one mole of an atom from the solid state to the gaseous state.
    {tex}{M_{(s)}} \to {M_{(g)}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\Delta _s}H(Sub\lim ation\,\,energy){/tex}
  2. Ionization: The energy required to take out electrons from one mole of atoms in the gaseous state. {tex}{M_{(g)}} \to {M^{2 + }} + 2e^- _{(g)}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\Delta _i}H(Ionization\,\,energy){/tex}
  3. Hydration: The energy released when one mole of ions are hydrated.
    {tex}{M^{2 + }}_{(g)} \to {M^{2 + }}_{(aq)}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\Delta _{hyd}}H(Hydration\,\,energy){/tex}
    Now, copper has a high energy of sublimation and very high sum of first two ionization energy values and low hydration energy. Hence, the Eo​​​​​​​(M2+/M) value for copper is positive.

question_text: How would you account for the irregular variation of ionization enthalpies (first and second) in the first series of the transition elements?
question_html: How would you account for the irregular variation of ionization enthalpies (first and second) in the first series of the transition elements?
solution_text: Due to an increase in nuclear charge which accompanies the filling of the inner d orbitals, there is a increase in ionisation enthalpy along each series of the transition elements from left to right. However, many small variations occur. The irregular trend in the first ionisation enthalpy of the 3d metals, can be accounted for by considering that the removal of one electron alters the relative energies of 4s and 3d orbitals

question_text: Which is a stronger reducing agent Cr 2+ or Fe 2+ and why?
question_html: Which is a stronger reducing agent Cr2+or Fe2+ and why?
solution_text: Reducing agents are those which itself get oxidised and make the other substance get reduced. The following reactions are involved when {tex} C{r^{2 + }}{/tex} and {tex}F{e^{2 + }}{/tex} act as reducing agents. {tex}C{r^{2 + }} \to C{r^{3+}} + e^- {/tex} and {tex}F{e^{2 + }} \to F{e^{3 + }}+e^-{/tex} . The {tex} E{^\circ _{C{r^{3 + }}/C{r^{2 + }}}}{/tex} value is - 0.41 V and {tex}E{^\circ _{F{e^{3 + }}/F{e^{2 + }}}}{/tex} is +0.77 V. This means that {tex}C{r^{2 + }}{/tex} can be easily oxidized to Cr 3+ , but Fe 2+ does not get oxidized to {tex}F{e^{3 + }}{/tex} easily. Therefore, {tex}C{r^{2 + }}{/tex} is a better reducing agent than {tex}F{e^{2 + }}{/tex} .

Reducing agents are those which itself get oxidised and make the other substance get reduced. The following reactions are involved when {tex} C{r^{2 + }}{/tex}and {tex}F{e^{2 + }}{/tex}act as reducing agents.
{tex}C{r^{2 + }} \to C{r^{3+}} + e^- {/tex} and  {tex}F{e^{2 + }} \to F{e^{3 + }}+e^-{/tex} . solution_text: Element M having atomic number(Z) = 27 will have electronic configuration as [Ar]3d 7 4s 2 . Therefore, the dipositive ion of M will have electronic configuration as [Ar]3d 7 . 3d 7 can be represented as follows: {tex}\uparrow{/tex} {tex}\downarrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex} {tex}\downarrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex} It has 3 unpaired electrons Therefore, n = 3 Magnetic moment can be calculated as {tex} = \sqrt {n\left( {n + 2} \right)} = \mu {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {3\left( {3 + 2} \right)} = \mu {/tex} {tex}= \sqrt {15} = \mu {/tex} {tex}\mu \approx 4BM{/tex}
solution_html:
Element M having atomic number(Z) = 27 will have electronic configuration as  [Ar]3d74s 2. Therefore, the dipositive ion of M will have electronic configuration as [Ar]3d7.
3d7 can be represented as follows:

{tex}\uparrow{/tex}{tex}\downarrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex}{tex}\downarrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex} {tex}\uparrow{/tex}

It has  3 unpaired electrons
Therefore, n = 3
Magnetic moment can be calculated as
{tex} = \sqrt {n\left( {n + 2} \right)} = \mu {/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt {3\left( {3 + 2} \right)} = \mu {/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt {15} = \mu {/tex}

question_text: Explain why Cu + ion is not stable in aqueous solutions?
question_html: Explain why Cu+ ion is not stable in aqueous solutions?
solution_text: In an aqueous medium,Cu 2 + is more stable than Cu + . This is because although high energy is required to remove one electron from Cu + to form,Cu 2 + as second ionization energy always more than first ionization energy. High hydration energy of Cu 2 + compensates for the high value of ionization energy. Therefore, Cu + ion in an aqueous solution is unstable. It disproportionates to give Cu 2 + and Cu. {tex}2C{u^ + }_{(aq)} \to C{u^{2 + }}_{(aq)} + C{u_{(s)}}{/tex}

question_text: Why are Mn 2+ compounds are more stable than Fe 2+ towards oxidation to their +3 state?
question_html: Why are Mn2+ compounds are more stable than Fe2+ towards oxidation to their +3 state?

Electronic configuration of {tex}F{e^{2 + }}{/tex} is {tex}{[Ar]^{18}}3{d^6}{/tex} .It is known that half-filled and fully-filled orbitals are more stable. This is the reason {tex}M{n^{2 + }}{/tex} shows resistance to oxidation to {tex}M{n^{3 + }}{/tex} .Also, {tex}F{e^{2 + }}{/tex} has {tex}3{d^6}{/tex} configuration and by losing one electron, its configuration changes to a more stable {tex}3{d^5}{/tex} configuration. Therefore, {tex}F{e^{2 + }}{/tex} easily gets oxidized to {tex}F{e^{ + 3}}{/tex} oxidation state. Therefore Mn +2 compounds are more stable than Fe +2 .

question_text: To what extent do the electronic configurations decide the stability of oxidation states in the first series of the transition elements? Illustrate your answer with examples.
question_html: To what extent do the electronic configurations decide the stability of oxidation states in the first series of the transition elements? Illustrate your answer with examples.
solution_text: Electronic configuration plays an important role in deciding the stability of oxidation state. Sc 3+ (3d 0 ) has stable electronic configuration (inert gas configuration), therefore Sc 3+ is more stable than Sc + . Similarly, Fe 3+ (3d 5 ) is more stable than Fe 2+ due to half filled d-orbitals in Fe 3+ configuration. Mn 2+ (3d 5 ) is more stable than Mn 3+ due to half filled d-orbitals in Mn +2 . V 5+ (3d 0 )is more stable than V 3+ due to stable inert gas electronic configuration in V +5 .

Similarly, Fe3+ (3d5) is more stable than Fe2+ due to half filled d-orbitals in Fe3+ configuration. Mn2+ (3d5) is more stable than Mn3+ due to half filled d-orbitals in Mn+2. V5+ (3d0)is more stable than V3+ due to stable inert gas electronic configuration in V+5.

question_text: What is lanthanoid contraction? What are the consequences of lanthanoid contraction?
question_html: What is lanthanoid contraction? What are the consequences of lanthanoid contraction?
solution_text: The decrease in atomic and ionic size with increase in atomic number in lanthanoids is called lanthanoid contraction. Its consequences are (i) It causes the radii of the members of the third transition series to be very similar to those of the corresponding members of the second series. The almost identical radii of Zr (160 pm) and Hf (159 pm), have almost similar sizes. (ii) Difficulty in separation of lanthanoids.
solution_html:
The decrease in atomic and ionic size with increase in atomic number in lanthanoids is called lanthanoid contraction. Its consequences are 

(i) It causes the radii of the members of the third transition series to be very similar to those of the corresponding members of the second series. The almost identical radii of Zr (160 pm) and Hf (159 pm), have almost similar sizes.

(ii) Difficulty in separation of lanthanoids.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_text: What are the characteristics of transition elements and why are they called transition elements? Which of the d-block elements may not be regarded as the transition elements?
question_html: What are the characteristics of transition elements and why are they called transition elements? Which of the d-block elements may not be regarded as the transition elements?
solution_text: Characteristics of transition elements: Most of the transition elements form coloured compounds. Their compounds are generally paramagnetic in nature. They have great tendency to form complexes. They show variable oxidation states. They are called transition elements because they are less electropositive than s-block elements and more electropositive than p-block elements. Transition elements should have incompletely filled d orbital in its ground state or more stable oxidation state. Zn, Cd, Hg are not regarded as transition elements because they have completely filled d orbitals.
solution_html:
Characteristics of transition elements:

  1. Most of the transition elements form coloured compounds.
  2. Their compounds are generally paramagnetic in nature.
  3. They have great tendency to form complexes.
  4. They show variable oxidation states.
    They are called transition elements because they are less electropositive than s-block elements and more electropositive than p-block elements. Transition elements should have incompletely filled d orbital in its ground state or more stable oxidation state.
    Zn, Cd, Hg are not regarded as transition elements because they have completely filled d orbitals.

question_text: In what way is the electronic configuration of the transition elements different from that of the non transition elements?
question_html: In what way is the electronic configuration of the transition elements different from that of the non transition elements?
solution_text: Transition elements contain incompletely filled d-subshell in pen-ultimate shell i.e. their electronic configuration is (n-1)d 1-10 ns 1-2 whereas non transition elements have no d-subshell or their d-subshell is completely filled and have ns 1-2 or ns 2 np 1-6 in their outermost shell.

question_text: Explain giving reasons: Transition metals and many of their compounds show paramagnetic behaviour. The enthalpies of atomisation of the transition metals are high. The transition metals generally form coloured compounds. Transition metals and their many compounds act as good catalyst.
question_html:
Explain giving reasons:

  1. Transition metals and many of their compounds show paramagnetic behaviour.
  2. The enthalpies of atomisation of the transition metals are high.
  3. The transition metals generally form coloured compounds.
  4. Transition metals and their many compounds act as good catalyst.
solution_text: Transition metals show paramagnetic behaviour. Paramagnetism arises due to the presence of one or more unpaired electrons in them. For example Mn(Z=25) has configuration 3d 5 4s 2 and has 5 unpaired electrons. Transition elements have high effective nuclear charge and a large number of valence electrons. They form strong metallic bonds. As the number of unpaired electrons increases strength of metallic bonding increases. When interatomic force increases, enthaply of atomization also increases.Therefore the enthalpy of atomization of transition metals is high. Most of the complexes of transition metals are coloured. This is because of the absorption of radiation from visible light region to promote an electron from lower energy d-orbitals to higher energy d orbital. In the presence of ligands, the d-orbitals split up into two sets of orbitals having different energies. Therefore, the transition of electrons can take place from lower energy set to higher energy set. The energy required for these transitions is quite small and falls in the visible region of radiation. The ions of transition metals absorb the radiation of a particular wavelength and get excited and on de excitation they emit light in visible region on spectrum, imparting colour to the solution. The catalytic activity of the transition elements can be explained by Their ability to show variable oxidation states and to form complexes- Transition metals form unstable intermediate compounds. Thus, they provide a new path with lower activation energy for the reaction. Transition metals also provide a suitable surface for the reactions to occur.

1. Transition metals show paramagnetic behaviour. Paramagnetism arises due to the presence of one or more unpaired electrons in them. For example Mn(Z=25) has configuration 3d5 4s2 and has 5 unpaired electrons.
  2. Transition elements have high effective nuclear charge and a large number of valence electrons. They form strong metallic bonds. As the number of unpaired electrons increases strength of metallic bonding increases. When interatomic force increases, enthaply of atomization also increases.Therefore the enthalpy of atomization of transition metals is high.
  3. Most of the complexes of transition metals are coloured. This is because of the absorption of radiation from visible light region to promote an electron from lower energy d-orbitals to higher energy d orbital. In the presence of ligands, the d-orbitals split up into two sets of orbitals having different energies. Therefore, the transition of electrons can take place from lower energy set to higher energy set. The energy required for these transitions is quite small and falls in the visible region of radiation. The ions of transition metals absorb the radiation of a particular wavelength and get excited and on de excitation they emit light in visible region on spectrum, imparting colour to the solution.
  4. The catalytic activity of the transition elements can be explained by
    1. Their ability to show variable oxidation states and to form complexes- Transition metals form unstable intermediate compounds. Thus, they provide a new path with lower activation energy for the reaction.
    2. Transition metals also provide a suitable surface for the reactions to occur.

question_text: What are interstitial compounds? Why are such compounds well known for transition metals?
question_html: What are interstitial compounds? Why are such compounds well known for transition metals?
solution_text: Interstitial compounds are those which are formed when small atoms like H, C or N are trapped inside the crystal lattices of metals. Transition metal have lots of voids in their crystal lattice in which smaller atoms can fit easily. Thus, because of the nature of the composition of transition metal they can easily form transition compounds.

question_text: Describe the preparation of potassium dichromate from iron chromite ore. What is the effect of increasing pH on a solution of potassium dichromate?
question_html: Describe the preparation of potassium dichromate from iron chromite ore. What is the effect of increasing pH on a solution of potassium dichromate?
solution_text: Potassium dichromate is prepared from chromite ore ​(FeCr 2 O 4 )​​​​​​ in the following steps. Step (1): Preparation of sodium chromate by fusion of chromite ore (FeCr 2 O 4 ) with sodium or potassium carbonate in free access of air. The reaction with sodium carbonate occurs as follows: {tex}4FeC{r_2}{O_4} + 8Na_2CO_3 + 7{O_2} \to 8N{a_2}Cr{_2O_4} + 2F{e_2}{O_3} + 8{CO_2}{/tex} Step (2): Conversion of sodium chromate into sodium dichromate- The yellow solution of sodium chromate is filtered and acidified with sulphuric acid to give a solution from which orange sodium dichromate, Na 2 Cr 2 O 7 . 2H 2 O can be crystallised. {tex}2N{a_2}Cr{O_4} + conc.{H_2}S{O_4} \to N{a_2}C{r_2}{O_7} + N{a_2}S{O_4} + {H_2}O{/tex} Step(3): Conversion of sodium dichromate to potassium dichromate - Sodium dichromate is more soluble than potassium dichromate. Therefore it is prepared by treating the solution of sodium dichromate with potassium chloride. {tex}N{a_2}C{r_2}{O_7} + 2KCl \to {K_2}C{r_2}{O_7} + 2NaCl{/tex} Potassium dichromate being less soluble than sodium chloride is obtained in the form of orange coloured crystals and can be removed by filtration. The chromates and dichromates are interconvertible in aqueous solution depending upon pH of the solution. 2 CrO 4 2– + 2H + → Cr 2 O 7 2– + H 2 O Cr 2 O 7 2– + 2 OH - → 2 CrO 4 2– + H 2 O On increasing pH of dichromate solution, dichromate will convert back to chromate ion.
solution_html:
Potassium dichromate is prepared from chromite ore ​(FeCr2O4)​​​​​​ in the following steps.
Step (1): Preparation of sodium chromate by fusion of chromite ore (FeCr2O4) with sodium or potassium carbonate in free access of air. The reaction with sodium
carbonate occurs as follows:
{tex}4FeC{r_2}{O_4} + 8Na_2CO_3 + 7{O_2} \to 8N{a_2}Cr{_2O_4} + 2F{e_2}{O_3} + 8{CO_2}{/tex}
Step (2): Conversion of sodium chromate into sodium dichromate- The yellow solution of sodium chromate is filtered and acidified with sulphuric acid to give a solution from which orange sodium dichromate, Na2Cr2O7. 2H2O can be crystallised.
{tex}2N{a_2}Cr{O_4} + conc.{H_2}S{O_4} \to N{a_2}C{r_2}{O_7} + N{a_2}S{O_4} + {H_2}O{/tex}
Step(3): Conversion of sodium dichromate to potassium dichromate - Sodium dichromate is more soluble than potassium dichromate. Therefore it is prepared by treating the solution of sodium dichromate with potassium chloride.
{tex}N{a_2}C{r_2}{O_7} + 2KCl \to {K_2}C{r_2}{O_7} + 2NaCl{/tex}
Potassium dichromate being less soluble than sodium chloride is obtained in the form of orange coloured crystals and can be removed by filtration.
The chromates and dichromates are interconvertible in aqueous solution depending upon pH of the solution. 
2 CrO42– + 2H+ → Cr2O72– + H2O
Cr2O72– + 2 OH- → 2 CrO42– + H2O
On increasing pH of dichromate solution, dichromate will convert back to chromate ion.

question_html:

  1. iodide
  2. iron(II) solution and
  3. H2S
solution_text: K 2 Cr 2 O 7 act as a very strong oxidising agent in the acidic medium. Cr 2 O 7 2– + 14H + + 6e - {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr 3+ + 7H 2 O (E 0 = 1.33V) . question_text: Describe the preparation of potassium permanganate. How does the acidified permanganate solution react with iron ions SO 2 oxalic acid? Write the ionic equations for the reactions.
question_html:
Describe the preparation of potassium permanganate. How does the acidified permanganate solution react with

  1. iron
  2. ions
  3. SO2
  4. oxalic acid?

Write the ionic equations for the reactions.
solution_text: Potassium permanganate can be prepared from pyrolusite {tex}\left( {Mn{O_2}} \right){/tex} .The ore is fused with KOH in the presence of either atmospheric oxygen or an oxidising agent, such as {tex}KN{O_3}{/tex} or {tex}KCl{O_4}{/tex} to give K 2 MnO 4 . {tex}2Mn{O_2} + 4KOH + {O_2}\xrightarrow{{heat}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {2{K_2}Mn{O_4}}\limits_{(Green)} + 2{H_2}O{/tex} This produces green colour K 2 MnO 4 which disproportionates in a neutral or acidic solution to give permanganate. 3MnO 4 2– + 4H + → 2MnO 4 - + MnO 2 + 2H 2 O Commercially it is prepared by the alkaline oxidative fusion of MnO 2 followed by the electrolytic oxidation of manganate (Vl). {tex}MnO_2\;\xrightarrow{fused\;with\;KOH,\;oxidised\;with\;KNO_3\;or\;air}MnO_4^{2-}{/tex} {tex}MnO_4^{2-}\;\xrightarrow{Electrolytic\;oxidation\;in\;alkaline\;solution}\;MnO_4^-{/tex} In the laboratory, manganese (II) ion salt is oxidised by peroxodisulphate to permanganate. 2Mn 2+ + 5S 2 O 8 2– + 8H 2 O → 2MnO 4 – + 10SO 4 2– + 16H + KMnO 4 act as a good oxidising agent in acidic medium. In acidic medium MnO 4 - itself get reduced to Mn 2+ and make the other substance get oxidised. Acidified {tex}KMn{O_4}{/tex} solution oxidizes Fe (II) ions to Fe (III) ions i.e., ferrous ions to ferric ions. Acidified potassium permanganate oxidizes {tex}S{O_2}{/tex} to sulphuric acid. Acidified potassium permanganate oxidizes oxalic acid to carbon dioxide.
solution_html:
Potassium permanganate can be prepared from pyrolusite {tex}\left( {Mn{O_2}} \right){/tex}.The ore is fused with KOH in the presence of either atmospheric oxygen or an oxidising agent, such as {tex}KN{O_3}{/tex} or {tex}KCl{O_4}{/tex} to give K2MnO4.
{tex}2Mn{O_2} + 4KOH + {O_2}\xrightarrow{{heat}}{/tex}{tex}\mathop {2{K_2}Mn{O_4}}\limits_{(Green)} + 2{H_2}O{/tex}
This produces green colour K2MnO4 which disproportionates in a neutral or acidic solution to give permanganate.

3MnO42– + 4H+ → 2MnO4- + MnO2 + 2H2O

Commercially it is prepared by the alkaline oxidative fusion of MnO2 followed by the electrolytic oxidation of manganate (Vl).

{tex}MnO_2\;\xrightarrow{fused\;with\;KOH,\;oxidised\;with\;KNO_3\;or\;air}MnO_4^{2-}{/tex}

{tex}MnO_4^{2-}\;\xrightarrow{Electrolytic\;oxidation\;in\;alkaline\;solution}\;MnO_4^-{/tex}

In the laboratory, manganese (II) ion salt is oxidised by peroxodisulphate to permanganate.
2Mn2+ + 5S2O82– + 8H2O → 2MnO4– + 10SO42– + 16H+

KMnO4 act as a good oxidising agent in acidic medium. In acidic medium MnO4- itself get reduced to Mn2+ and make the other substance get oxidised.

  1.  Acidified {tex}KMn{O_4}{/tex} solution oxidizes Fe (II) ions to Fe (III) ions i.e., ferrous ions to ferric ions.
  2. Acidified potassium permanganate oxidizes {tex}S{O_2}{/tex} to sulphuric acid.
  3. Acidified potassium permanganate oxidizes oxalic acid to carbon dioxide.

question_text: For M 2+ /M and M 3 + /M 2+ systems, the E o values for some metals are as follows: Cr 2+ /Cr = -0.9 V Cr 3+ /Cr = -0.4 V Mn 2+ /Mn = -1.2 V Mn 3+ /Mn 2+ = +1.5 V Fe 2+ /Fe= -0.4 V Fe 3+ /Fe 2+ = +0.8 V. Use this data to comment upon: The stability of Fe 3+ in acid solution as compared to that of Cr 3+ or Mn 3+ The ease with which iron can be oxidised as compared to a similar process for either chromium or manganese metal.
question_html:
For M2+/M and M3+/M2+ systems, the Eo values for some metals are as follows:
Cr2+/Cr = -0.9 V
Cr3+/Cr = -0.4 V
Mn2+/Mn = -1.2 V
Mn3+/Mn2+ = +1.5 V
Fe2+/Fe= -0.4 V
Fe3+/Fe2+ = +0.8 V.
Use this data to comment upon:

  1. The stability of Fe3+ in acid solution as compared to that of Cr3+ or Mn3+
  2. The ease with which iron can be oxidised as compared to a similar process for either chromium or manganese metal.
solution_text: The oxidation potential is a measure of the tendency of a chemical species to undergo oxidation. More is the tendency of a substance to undergo oxidation, more will be its oxidation potential. Similarly, the reduction potential is a measure of the tendency of a chemical species to undergo reduction. More is the tendency of a substance to undergo reduction, more will be its reduction potential and lesser will be its oxidation potential. Oxidation potential = - (Reduction Potential) The E o value (Standard reduction potential value) for {tex}F{e^{3 + }}/F{e^{2 + }}{/tex} is higher than that for {tex}C{r^{3 + }}/C{r^{2 + }}{/tex} and lower than that for {tex}Mn^{3+}/Mn^{2+}{/tex} . So, the reduction of {tex}F{e^{3 + }}to\,F{e^{2 + }}{/tex} is easier than the reduction of {tex}Mn^{3+}\,to\,Mn^{2+}{/tex} , but not as easy as the reduction of {tex}C{r^{3 + }}{/tex} to {tex}C{r^{2 + }}{/tex} .Hence, {tex}F{e^{3 + }}{/tex} is more stable than {tex}M{n^{3 + }}{/tex} , but less stable than {tex}C{r^{3 + }}{/tex} . The stability of Cr 3+ is because of the presence of a half-filled t 2g configuration in Cr 3+ . These metal ions can be arranged in the increasing order of their stability as {tex}M{n^{3 + }} < F{e^{3 + }} < C{r^{3 + }}{/tex} . The reduction potentials for the given pairs increase in the following order. {tex}M{n^{2 + }}/Mn < C{r^{2 + }}/Cr < F{e^{2 + }}/Fe{/tex} So, the oxidation of Fe to {tex}F{e^{2 + }}{/tex} is not as easy as the oxidation of Cr to {tex}C{r^{2 + }}{/tex} and the oxidation of Mn to {tex}M{n^{2 + }}{/tex} Thus, these metals can be arranged in the increasing order of their ability to get oxidised as Fe < Cr < Mn.

question_text: Compare the stability of +2 oxidation state for all the elements of the first transition series.
question_html: Compare the stability of +2 oxidation state for all the elements of the first transition series.
solution_text: Sc +3 Ti +1 +2 +3 +4 V +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 Cr +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 +6 Mn +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 +6 +7 Fe +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 +6 Co +1 +2 +3 +4 +5 Ni +1 +2 +3 +4 Cu +1 +2 +3 The elements which give the greatest number of oxidation states occur in or near the middle of the series. Manganese, for example, exhibits all the oxidation states from +2 to +7. The lesser number of oxidation states at the extreme ends stems from either too few electrons to lose or share (Sc, Ti) or too many d electrons (hence fewer orbitals available in which to share electrons with others) for higher valence (Cu, Zn). Thus, early in the series scandium(II) is virtually unknown and titanium (IV) is more stable than Ti(III) or Ti(II). At the other end, the only oxidation state of zinc is +2 (no d electrons are involved). The relative stability of the +2 oxidation state increases on moving from Sc to Zn. This is because on moving from top to bottom, it becomes more and more difficult to remove the third electron from the d-orbital.

question_text: Compare the chemistry of actinoids with that of the lanthanoids with special reference to: electronic configuration oxidation state atomic and ionic sizes and chemical reactivity.
question_html:
Compare the chemistry of actinoids with that of the lanthanoids with special reference to:

  1. electronic configuration
  2. oxidation state
  3. atomic and ionic sizes and
  4. chemical reactivity.
solution_text: Electronic configuration - The general electronic configuration for lanthanoids is {tex}{[Xe]^{54}}4{f^{1 - 14}}5{d^{0 - 1}}6{s^2}{/tex} and that for actinoids is {tex}{[Rn]^{86}}5{f^{1 - 14}}6{d^{0 - 1}}7{s^2}{/tex} . Unlike 4f orbitals, 5f orbitals are not deeply buried and participate in bonding to a greater extent. Oxidation states - The principal oxidation state of lanthanoids is (+3). However, sometimes we also encounter oxidation states of + 2 and + 4. This is because of extra stability of fully-filled and half-filled orbitals. Ce (IV) formation is favoured due to extra stability of noble gas configuration, but it is a strong oxidant reverting to the common +3 state . Pr, Nd, Tb and Dy also exhibit +4 state but only in oxides, MO 2 Eu 2+ is formed by losing the two s- electrons. On the other hand, actinoids exhibit a greater range of oxidation states. This is because the 5f, 6d, and 7s levels are of comparable energies. Again, (+3) is the principal oxidation state for actinoids. Actinoids such as lanthanoids have more compounds in +3 state than in +4 state. Atomic and ionic sizes - The overall decrease in atomic and ionic radii from lanthanum to lutetium is a unique feature in the chemistry of the lanthanoids and is termed as Lanthanoid contraction. Similar to lanthanoids, actinoids also exhibit actinoid contraction (overall decrease in atomic and ionic radii). The contraction is greater due to the poor shielding effect of 5f orbitals. Chemical reactivity - In the lanthanide series, the earlier members of the series are more reactive. They have reactivity that is comparable to Ca. With an increase in the atomic number, the lanthanides start behaving similarly to Al. Lanthanide combines with hydrogen when gently heated in the gas. The carbides, Ln 3 C, Ln 2 C 3 and LnC 2 are formed when the metals are heated with carbon. They liberate hydrogen from dilute acids and burn in halogens to form halides. They form oxides M 2 O 3 and hydroxides M(OH) 3 . The hydroxides are definite compounds, not just hydrated oxides. They are basic like alkaline earth metal oxides and hydroxides. Actinoids, on the other hand, are highly reactive metals, especially when they are finely divided. When they are added to boiling water, they give a mixture of oxide and hydride. Actinoids combine with most of the non-metals at moderate temperatures. Alkalies have no action on these actinoids. In the case of acids, they are slightly affected by nitric acid (because of the formation of a protective oxide layer).

1. Electronic configuration- The general electronic configuration for lanthanoids is {tex}{[Xe]^{54}}4{f^{1 - 14}}5{d^{0 - 1}}6{s^2}{/tex} and that for actinoids is {tex}{[Rn]^{86}}5{f^{1 - 14}}6{d^{0 - 1}}7{s^2}{/tex}. Unlike 4f orbitals, 5f orbitals are not deeply buried and participate in bonding to a greater extent.
  2. Oxidation states- The principal oxidation state of lanthanoids is (+3). However, sometimes we also encounter oxidation states of + 2 and + 4.   3. Atomic and ionic sizes- The overall decrease in atomic and ionic radii from lanthanum to lutetium is a unique feature in the chemistry of the lanthanoids and is termed as Lanthanoid contraction. Similar to lanthanoids, actinoids also exhibit actinoid contraction (overall decrease in atomic and ionic radii). The contraction is greater due to the poor shielding effect of 5f orbitals.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_text: How would you account for the following: Of the d 4 species, Cr 2+ is strongly reducing while manganese(III) is strongly oxidising. Cobalt (II) is stable in an aqueous solution but in the presence of complexing reagents, it is easily oxidized. The d 1 configuration is very unstable in ions.
question_html:
How would you account for the following:

  1. Of the d4 species, Cr2+ is strongly reducing while manganese(III) is strongly oxidising.
  2. Cobalt (II) is stable in an aqueous solution but in the presence of complexing reagents, it is easily oxidized.
  3. The d1 configuration is very unstable in ions.
solution_text: Cr 2+ is strongly reducing because it changes to Cr 3+ which is more stable because Cr 3+ has a stable half-filled t 2g configuration whereas Mn 3+ is strongly oxidizing because it changes to Mn 2+ which is more stable due to half-filled d-orbitals. Co (III) being more stable than Co(II), the change in the oxidation state of cobalt from +2 to +3 is easy in presence of the complexing agent. d 1 configuration is very unstable in ions because after losing one more electron it will become stable. All elements with d 1 configuration are either reducing or undergo disproportionation. e.g. {tex}\mathop {\mathop {3MnO_4^{2 - }}\limits_{ + 6} }\limits_{3{d^1}} + 4{H^ + } \to \mathop {\mathop {2MnO_4^ - }\limits_{ + 7} }\limits_{3d^\circ } + \mathop {Mn{O_2}}\limits_{ + 4} + 2{H_2}O{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Cr2+ is strongly reducing because it changes to Cr3+ which is more stable because Cr3+ has a stable half-filled t2g configuration whereas Mn3+ is strongly oxidizing because it changes to Mn2+ which is more stable due to half-filled d-orbitals.
  2. Co (III) being more stable than Co(II), the change in the oxidation state of cobalt from +2 to +3 is easy in presence of the complexing agent.
  3. d1 configuration is very unstable in ions because after losing one more electron it will become stable. All elements with d1 configuration are either reducing or undergo disproportionation. e.g.
    {tex}\mathop {\mathop {3MnO_4^{2 - }}\limits_{ + 6} }\limits_{3{d^1}} + 4{H^ + } \to \mathop {\mathop {2MnO_4^ - }\limits_{ + 7} }\limits_{3d^\circ } + \mathop {Mn{O_2}}\limits_{ + 4} + 2{H_2}O{/tex}

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_text: Give examples and suggest reasons for the following features of the transition metal chemistry: The lowest oxide of transition metal is basic, the highest is amphoteric/acidic. A transition metal exhibits highest oxidation state in oxides and fluorides. The highest oxidation state is exhibited in oxoanions of a metal.
question_html:
Give examples and suggest reasons for the following features of the transition metal chemistry:

  1. The lowest oxide of transition metal is basic, the highest is amphoteric/acidic.
  2. A transition metal exhibits highest oxidation state in oxides and fluorides.
  3. The highest oxidation state is exhibited in oxoanions of a metal.
solution_text: The lower oxide of transition metal is basic because the metal atom has low oxidation state and hence metal has low charge density whereas highest is acidic due to highest oxidation state. Highest oxidation state increases charge density on metal and thus acidity increases. In the low oxidation state of the metal, some of the valence electrons of the metal atom are not involved in bonding. Hence, it can donate electrons and behave as a base. In the higher oxidation state valence electrons are involved in the bonding and are not available. Instead effective nuclear charge is high. Hence, it can accept electrons and hence behave as acid. A transition metal exhibits higher oxidation states in oxides and fluorides because oxygen and fluorine are highly electronegative elements, small in size and strongest oxidizing agents thus can oxidise metal to highest oxidation state. For example, osmium shows an oxidation states of +6 in O s F 6 and vanadium shows an oxidation state of +5 in V 2 O 5 . Oxometal anions have highest oxidation state, e.g. Cr in {tex}C{r_2}O_7^{2 - }{/tex} or {tex}CrO_4^{2 - }{/tex} has an oxidation state of +6 whereas Mn in {tex}MnO_4^ -{/tex} has an oxidation state of +7. This is again due to the combination of metal with oxygen, which is highly electronegative and good oxidizing element.

question_html: What are alloys? Name an important alloy which contains some of the Lanthanoid metals. Mention its uses.
solution_text: Alloys are homogenous mixture of two or more metals. One of them can be non metal also. Mischmetal is an alloy which contains some of the Lanthanoid metals, it contains 95% Lanthanoid metals and iron ~ 5% and traces of S, C, Ca and Al. Mischmetal is used in Mg-based alloy to produce bullets, shell and lighter flint. Addition of 3% mischmetal to magnesium increases its strength and used in making jet engine parts.

question_text: The chemistry of the actinoid elements is not so smooth as that of the Lanthanoids. Justify this statement by giving some examples from the oxidation state of these elements.
question_html: The chemistry of the actinoid elements is not so smooth as that of the Lanthanoids. Justify this statement by giving some examples from the oxidation state of these elements.
solution_text: Lanthanoids primarily show three oxidation states (+2, +3, +4). Among these oxidation states, +3 state is the most common. Lanthanoids display a limited number of oxidation states because the energy difference between 4f, 5d, and 6s orbitals is quite large. On the other hand, the energy difference between 5f, 6d, and 7s orbitals is very less so, actinoids shows large variety of oxidation state. The elements, in the first half of the series of actinoids frequently exhibit higher oxidation states. For example, the maximum oxidation state increases from +4 in Th to +5, +6 and +7 respectively in Pa, U and Np but decreases in succeeding elements. The actinoids resemble the lanthanoids in having more compounds in +3 state than in the +4 state. However, +3 and +4 ions tend to hydrolyse. Because the distribution of oxidation states among the actinoids is so uneven and so different for the former and later elements, it is unsatisfactory to review their chemistry in terms of oxidation states.

chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_text: Compare the general characteristics of the first series of the transition metals with those of the second and third series metals in the respective vertical columns. Give special emphasis on the following points: Electronic configurations Oxidation states Ionisation enthalpies and Atomic sizes.
question_html:
Compare the general characteristics of the first series of the transition metals with those of the second and third series metals in the respective vertical columns. Give special emphasis on the following points:

  1. Electronic configurations
  2. Oxidation states
  3. Ionisation enthalpies and
  4. Atomic sizes.
solution_text: Electronic configuration : In 1 st transition series, 3d orbitals are progressively filled whereas in second transition series, 4d orbitals are progressively filled and in 3 rd transition series 5d-orbitals are progressively filled. Oxidation states: Elements show variable oxidation states in both the series. The highest oxidation state is equal to total number of 's' as well as 'd' orbitals. The number of oxidation states shown are less in 5d transition series than 4d series. In 3d series + 2, +3 oxidation states are common and they form stable complexes in these oxidation states. In other series O s O 4 and PtF 6 are formed which are quite stable in higher oxidation state. Ionisation enthalpies: The ionization enthalpy of 5d series is higher than 3d and 4d series due to lanthanoid contraction, effective nuclear charge increases. Atomic size: The atomic size of 4d and 5d series do not differ appreciably due to lanthanoid contraction. The atomic radii of second and third series are larger than 3d series.

  3. Ionisation enthalpies: The ionization enthalpy of 5d series is higher than 3d and 4d series due to lanthanoid contraction, effective nuclear charge increases.
  4. Atomic size: The atomic size of 4d and 5d series do not differ appreciably due to lanthanoid contraction. The atomic radii of second and third series are larger than 3d series.

v. Fe2+ 3d6 6 4 {tex}\mathrm{t}^{4}_{2 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{e}^{2}_{\mathrm{g}}{/tex}
vi. Fe3+ 3d5 5 5 {tex}\mathrm{t}^{3}_{2 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{e}^{2}_{\mathrm{g}}{/tex}
vii. Co2+ 3d7 7 3 {tex}\mathrm{t}^{5}_{2 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{e}^{2}_{\mathrm{g}}{/tex}
viii. Ni2+ 3d8 8 2 {tex}\mathrm{t}^{6}_{2 \mathrm{g}} \mathrm{e}_{2 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}
ix. Cu2+ 3d9 9 1 {tex}\mathrm{t}^{6}_{2 \mathrm{g}} \mathrm{e}_{3 \mathrm{g}}{/tex}

Note: In an octahedral field, the d-orbitals split into two sets of orbitals, the set of orbitals (dxy,dyz,dxz) with lower energy is called t2g and the set of orbitals (dx2-y2 and dz2) with higher energy is called eg.

subject: Chemistry
chapter: The d- and f- Block Elements
question_text: Comment on the statement that elements of the first transition series possess many properties different from those of heavier transition elements.
question_html: Comment on the statement that elements of the first transition series possess many properties different from those of heavier transition elements.
solution_text: In first transition series lower oxidation state is more stable whereas in heavier transition elements higher oxidation states are more stable. The ionization enthalpy of 5d transition series is higher than 3d and 4d transition series. M-M bonding is most common in heavier transition metals but less in first series. The elements of first transition series do not form complexes with higher coordination number of 7 and 8. The elements of first series can form high spin or low spin complexes depending upon strength of ligands but elements of outer series from low spin complex irrespective of strength of ligands.
solution_html:
1. In first transition series lower oxidation state is more stable whereas in heavier transition elements higher oxidation states are more stable.
  2. The ionization enthalpy of 5d transition series is higher than 3d and 4d transition series.
  3. M-M bonding is most common in heavier transition metals but less in first series.
  4. The elements of first transition series do not form complexes with higher coordination number of 7 and 8.
  5. The elements of first series can form high spin or low spin complexes depending upon strength of ligands but elements of outer series from low spin complex irrespective of strength of ligands.

question_text: What can be inferred from the magnetic moment values of the following complex species? Example Magnetic moment (i) [K 4 [Mn(CN) 6 ] 2.2 (ii) [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ 5.3 (iii) K 2 [MnCl 4 ] 5.9
question_html:
What can be inferred from the magnetic moment values of the following complex species?

Example Magnetic moment
(i) [K4 [Mn(CN)6] 2.2
(ii) [Fe(H2O)6]2+ 5.3
(iii) K2 [MnCl4] 5.9
solution_text: K 4 [Mn(CN) 6 ] For in transition metals, the magnetic moment is calculated from the spin-only formula. Therefore, {tex}\sqrt{n(n+2)}=2.2{/tex} In this complex, Mn is in the +2 oxidation state. This means that Mn has 5 electrons in the d-orbital. Hence, we can say that CN - is a strong field ligand that causes the pairing of electrons. [Fe(H 2 O) 6 ] 2+ {tex}\sqrt{n(n+2)}=5.3{/tex} In this complex, Fe is in the +2 oxidation state. This means that Fe has 6 electrons in the d-orbital. Hence, we can say that H 2 O is a weak field ligand and does not cause the pairing of electrons. K 2 [MnCl 4 ] {tex}\sqrt{n(n+2)}=5.9{/tex} In this complex, Mn is in the +2 oxidation state. This means that Mn has 5 electrons in the d-orbital. Hence, we can say that Cl - is a weak field ligand and does not cause the pairing of electrons.
solution_html:
1. K4[Mn(CN)6]
    For in transition metals, the magnetic moment is calculated from the spin-only formula. Therefore, {tex}\sqrt{n(n+2)}=2.2{/tex}
     In this complex, Mn is in the +2 oxidation state. This means that Mn has 5 electrons in the d-orbital.
    Hence, we can say that CN- is a strong field ligand that causes the pairing of electrons.
  2. [Fe(H2O)6]2+
    {tex}\sqrt{n(n+2)}=5.3{/tex}
    In this complex, Fe is in the +2 oxidation state. This means that Fe has 6 electrons in the d-orbital.
    Hence, we can say that H2O is a weak field ligand and does not cause the pairing of electrons.
  3. K2[MnCl4]
    {tex}\sqrt{n(n+2)}=5.9{/tex}
    In this complex, Mn is in the +2 oxidation state. This means that Mn has 5 electrons in the d-orbital.
    Hence, we can say that Cl- is a weak field ligand and does not cause the pairing of electrons.
